title: Black Box Data After a Semi Crash in Chattanooga, Tennessee
twitter: 🚛 Semi crash in Chattanooga? The truck's BLACK BOX data can disappear within DAYS. Tennessee gives you 2 years — but evidence won't wait. Call 855-WRECKMATCH (855-897-3256) NOW. https://www.wreckmatch.com/blog/black-box-data-after-a-semi-crash-in-chattanooga-tennessee
linkedin:
If you or a client has been involved in a semi-truck crash in Chattanooga, Tennessee, one of the most critical — and time-sensitive — pieces of evidence is the truck's black box, also known as the ECM or EDR.

Commercial trucks record data including speed, braking, engine performance, and hours of service in the moments before a crash. This data can make or break a liability case. The problem? Trucking companies are not legally obligated to preserve it indefinitely, and it can be overwritten or lost within days of a collision.

Tennessee's 2-year statute of limitations may seem like plenty of time, but waiting even a few weeks can mean critical evidence is gone forever. Preservation letters and spoliation notices must be sent immediately after a crash to protect your client's rights.

At WreckMatch, we connect injured victims with attorneys from our network of 800+ law firms who specialize in complex truck accident litigation — including cases where ECM/EDR data is central to proving fault.

facebook:
If a loved one was hit by a semi-truck in Chattanooga, Tennessee, we know how overwhelming and frightening that experience can be. You're dealing with injuries, insurance calls, and so much uncertainty — and it feels like everything is happening at once.

Here's something most people don't know: that semi-truck likely has a black box — called an ECM or EDR — that recorded exactly what the driver was doing before the crash. Speed, braking, engine data, even hours of service. It could be the most powerful evidence in your case.

But here's the urgent part — that data can be erased or overwritten within days. Once it's gone, it's gone. Tennessee does give you two years to file a claim, but the evidence won't wait two years.

The right attorney can send an immediate preservation letter to make sure that black box data is protected before the trucking company has a chance to wipe it.

Have you or someone you know been in a semi-truck accident and didn't know evidence like this even existed?
reddit_title: Semi truck hit me in Chattanooga, TN — what is black box data and can it help my case?
reddit_body:
If you were recently involved in a semi-truck crash in Chattanooga, Tennessee, one of the first things you should know is that commercial trucks are equipped with what's called a black box — technically an ECM (Engine Control Module) or EDR (Event Data Recorder). This device records crucial data like vehicle speed, brake application, throttle position, and hours of service in the time leading up to a collision. That data can be incredibly powerful evidence when proving fault.

The most important thing to understand is that this data is not preserved automatically or indefinitely. Trucking companies have no legal obligation to keep it unless they receive a formal preservation or spoliation letter from an attorney. In many cases, the data gets overwritten within days. Tennessee has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims, but waiting even a few weeks can mean losing this evidence permanently. If you suspect a truck's black box data is relevant to your case, you need an attorney to act fast.

For anyone navigating this situation, getting connected with a lawyer who handles commercial truck litigation specifically — not just general personal injury — makes a real difference. Sites like wreckmatch.com can help match you with attorneys experienced in truck accident cases in Tennessee, which is worth checking out if you're not sure where to start.
